title: Evolution of the intertwining correlated topological phases in iron-based superconductor Fe(Te,Se)
authors: Yue Sun, Shiying He, Zhongyi Zhang, Yong Huang, Jingheng Chen, Weixiang Yan, Chunbo Yu, Yuyang Dong, Kohei Aido, Xin Zhou, Zhengtai Liu, Mao Ye, Jishan Liu, Haruhisa Kitano, Zhixiang Shi, Hong Ding, Takeshi Kondo, Xianxin Wu, Peng Zhang
abstract: Multiple topological electronic phases can coexist within a single quantum material and induce different topological superconducting states, offering deeper insights into interplay of topological superconducting states and Majorana modes, which may also be influenced and modified by correlation effect. Iron-based superconductors, with both topological states and correlation effect, is an ideal platform to study these phenomena. Here, with high resolution angle resolved photoelectron spectroscopy, we directly resolve two distinct intertwining topological states in iron-based superconductor Co-doped Fe(Te,Se), and study their evolution with electron doping. We identify a region where both topological insulator surface states and topological Dirac semimetal states intersect the Fermi level. The topological states are affected by the strong correlation effect and are isolated from trivial bulk states. The evolution between distinct topological phases offers a good opportunity to study various Majorana modes from different superconducting phases according to theoretical analysis. Our findings establish an ideal platform for exploring the interaction between multiple topological superconducting states and the related Majorana modes.
summary: This work uses ARPES on Co-doped iron-based superconductors to study how multiple topological electronic states coexist and interact under electron doping. The key finding is the direct observation of two distinct topological bands intersecting at the Fermi level, which establishes the material as an excellent platform for exploring complex Majorana mode physics in correlated superconductors.
main_problem: The study investigates the complex interplay between multiple coexisting topological superconducting states and strong correlation effects within iron-based superconductors.
main_result: The authors directly resolved two distinct intertwining topological states (TI surface and TDS bulk) in Co-doped Fe(Te,Se), showing that their intersection at the Fermi level enables the existence of multiple Majorana modes.
method: The research employed high-resolution Angle-Resolved Photoelectron Spectroscopy (ARPES) to map the band structure evolution, supplemented by X-ray diffraction and magnetic susceptibility measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system is the iron-based superconductor $	ext{Fe}(	ext{Te},	ext{Se})$, specifically $	ext{Co}$-doped $	ext{Fe}(	ext{Te},	ext{Se})$, which serves as an ideal platform to study correlated topological superconductivity.
key_observables: Band dispersion, Dirac point positions (TI and TDS), superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) dependence on doping, and the presence/evolution of Majorana zero modes.
important_parameters: Electron doping level ($x$ via Co substitution), Fermi level position ($E_F$), and the energy separation between the two topological bands ($\approx 20 	ext{ meV}$).
main_assumptions: The topological states observed are significantly affected by the strong correlation effect, and the $	ext{Co}$ substitution successfully tunes the system to isolate the relevant topological physics.
figures_summary: Figures show the evolution of TI and TDS Dirac bands with Co doping, confirming their relative positions and the shift of Dirac points. Other figures illustrate the $k_z$ dispersion and schematic representations of hybrid Majorana vortex phases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first characterizing the material structure and doping dependence (Fig. 1), then using ARPES to map the coexisting topological bands (Fig. 2), and finally analyzing the implications for Majorana modes by tracking band evolution and theoretical modeling (Fig. 3, 4).
why_interesting: The explicit focus on correlated topological superconductivity and the detailed mapping of multiple interacting topological states (TI/TDS) provides a rich playground for understanding emergent quantum phenomena beyond simple BCS theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Structure, composition and Tc of Co-doped FeTe0.6Se0.4. a, Lattice constant c for Fe1−xCoxTe0.6Se0.4 (0 ≤x ≤0.4) obtained from the X-ray diffraction measure- ments. b, EDX results for the actual Co concentrations. c, Temperature dependence of the reduced magnetic susceptibil- ities -χ/χ(2 K) under 5 Oe for Fe1−xCoxTe0.6Se0.4 (0 ≤x ≤ 0.15). d, Sketch of band dispersion along kz direction. There are two band inversions (Band inversion I and II). α/β band consists of dxz/yz orbital, while γ band mainly consists of pz orbital. In undoped Fe(Te,Se), Fermi level is located roughly at the dashed line, in the SOC gap of the band inversion I.

Fig. 2. Evolution of the TI and TDS Dirac bands with Co doping. a-d, Band structure of Fe1−xCoxTe0.6Se0.4 (x = 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15) near Γ at 30 K, with a laser delivering p-polarized 7-eV photons. e-h, Curvature intensity plots of a-d. i, Shift of the TI (I) and TDS (II) Dirac points with Co doping. Extraction method of the Dirac point positions can be found in Supplementary Information, Section S2. j, Sketch of the in-plane band structure along ΓM. The Dirac band (I) with red and blue lines is the TI Dirac band. The Dirac band (II) on top is the TDS band. The dashed lines represent the corresponding Fermi level at different Co doping.

Fig. 3. Photon-energy dependence of band structure in Fe1−xCoxTe0.6Se0.4 (x = 0.1 and 0.15) at 30K. a, ARPES spectra of x = 0.1 sample at different photon energies from 30 eV to 44 eV. b, The same as a but with x = 0.15 sample. c, EDC curvature plot of a. The bulk Dirac point is determined to be at around 36 eV. The black lines indicate the bulk bands. The blue and red dashed lines in c(i) indicated the TI surface band and TDS surface components, which are unclear at higher photon energies.

Fig. 4. Possible Majorana modes in electron-doped Fe(Te,Se). a, Schematic surface spectrum of the (001) surface in the normal state. b, Hybrid vortex phase around EF1 with conventional s-wave pairing, exhibiting both Majorana zero mode (yellow) at the end of vortex and 1D Majorana mode (cyan) along the vortex. c, Calculated local density of states of the two Majorana modes in b. d, e, Majorana mode in the vortex (purple) and Majorana mode at the hinge (green) with B1u/2u pairing around EF2. d shows the distribution of the two Majorana modes in real space, and e shows their calculated local density of states. f, When the Majorana mode in the vortex is close to a hinge, the two Majorana modes...
